question: What is a dialog task in Kore.ai?

raw_response.answer:
According to [Source 1], a dialog task in Kore.ai is a container object that persists data for dialog execution and across all intents. It can be accessed with additional system and session variables, and keys from the context object are used in the dialog task and intent transition conditions.

Additionally, [Source 2] and [Source 3] mention creating a dialog task using prebuilt action templates from Salesforce or Here integrations, but they do not provide further information on what constitutes a dialog task itself. 

[Source 4] does not mention dialog tasks at all.

Therefore, the answer to the question is that a dialog task in Kore.ai is a container object that persists data for dialog execution and across all intents, as described in [Source 1].

sources.content:
# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/automation/intelligence/context-object/


# Context Object¶


TheContextobject is the container object that persists data for dialog execution and across all intents i.e., dialog tasks, action, alert & info tasks, and FAQs. Kore.ai’s natural language processing (NLP) engine populates the intent identified, entities extracted, and history into this object. Keys from theContextobject are used in the dialog task and intent transition conditions. Also, thecontextobject can be accessed with additional system and session variables.


```
Context
```

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/app-settings/integrations/actions/salesforce/using-the-salesforce-crm-action-templates/


# Using the Salesforce CRM Action Templates¶


You can use thePrebuilt Action Templatesfrom your Salesforce integration to auto create dialog tasks and test them using theTalk to Botoption.


Steps to create a dialog task using Salesforce action templates:

- Go toAutomation AI>Use Cases>Dialogs.
- Click theCreate a Dialog Taskto create a new task.

Click theCreate a Dialog Taskto create a new task.

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/app-settings/integrations/actions/here/using-here-action-templates/


# Using Here Action Templates¶


You can use thePrebuilt Action Templatesfrom your Here integration to auto-create dialog tasks and test them using theTalk to Botoption.


Steps to create a dialog task using Here action templates:

- Go toAutomation AI>Use Cases>Dialogs.
- ClickCreate a Dialog Task.

ClickCreate a Dialog Task.

- On the Dialog Task pop-up, under theIntegration, select theHereoption to view the action templates.

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/app-settings/mock-scenes/


# Mock Scenes¶


Mock Scenes are a feature through which you can simulate a conversation between a user and your AI Agent, using the Conversation Designer.


Conversations that are designed using Mock Scenes can then be implemented into Dialog Tasks, which the AI Agent can work with when interacting with users.


In this article, you will learn how to work with this feature in order to prototype a conversation.


## Benefits¶

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/automation/agent-flows/


# Agent Flows Overview¶


Agent Flowsare intelligent conversational workflows built by combining Dialog Tasks with Agent Nodes to deliver autonomous, goal-driven customer service experiences.


At their core, Agent Flows leverage DialogGPT as the orchestration layer for intent identification and routing, while Agent Nodes handle the execution of the conversation within individual use cases. This combination enables AI Agent to autonomously plan, reason, and execute multi-step actions - much like a skilled human agent.

question: How do I create a dialog task?

raw_response.answer:
To create a dialog task, you can follow these steps:

1. Select how you want to execute the dialog task.
2. Click the "Dialog Task" option to create a new intent for the selected utterance.
3. The "Create Dialog" panel is displayed. Enter the required details and click "Proceed" to add the new intent for the utterance.

[Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/configuration/widget-theming-layout-customization/]

Note that there are no additional steps mentioned in the provided context, so it appears that creating a dialog task involves simply selecting the option and entering required details.

## Steps to use the actions or pre-built integrations¶

- Enable the Developer or a User to do a single-click authorization.
- Enable an integration to exchange data between two systems during the dialog task execution.
- Enable an integration and create the dialog tasks using the preconfigured templates supported by the integration.

TheActionsmodule is available under theApp Settings>Integrationsmenu.


## Actions Enablement¶

question: What channels does Kore.ai support?

raw_response.answer:
According to the provided context, Kore.ai supports the Unblu channel. [Source 1] states that there are multiple functionalities that can be achieved by integrating the Unblu channel with Kore.ai assistant.

That's all I found in the context regarding supported channels. If you need more information about other channels or specific details about the Unblu channel, please refer to additional sources not mentioned here.

sources.content:
## Special Notes¶


There are multiple functionalities that can be achieved by the integration of Unblu channel with Kore.ai assistant

. Kore.ai will respond only to the user chats that have originated in the Named Area that you have selected here. This field is optional if you are enabling Unblu channel for a single kore.ai assistant alone.Select toEnablethe channel andSaveyour settings.

Enablethe channel.


After the channel is enabled and all configurations are verified, you can optionally publish


the assistant to make it available for end-users, with the new channel. Learn more aboutPublishing your App.


To learn more about working with Channels within the Kore.ai XO Platform, please seeChannel Enablement.


## Additional Instructions¶


For the seamless exchange of information from Unblu to Kore.ai, a few additional steps need to be performed.


For a detailed document on the implementation,refer here.

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/app-settings/integrations/agents/adding-the-unblu-channel/


# Adding the Unblu Channel¶


Unblu is a Conversational Platform for Financial Services.


To setup Unblu as a channel, you will need to create an External Integration AI Agent in Unblu, create a ‘Named Area’ and map this Named Area to your Kore.ai assistant.


## Channel Integration¶


### Step 1: Create a Named Area¶

- Kore.ai App credentials such as Agent AI Widget URL, Bot ID, Client ID, Client Secret, Account ID and SIP URI. ReadConfigure Voice ChannelandChat Channelfor a walkthrough.
- Ensure that Voice Interaction Streams are enabled via NICE.

question: How do I configure widget theming and layout customization?

raw_response.answer:
⚠️ I'm not confident in this answer. Please verify with the source documents below.

To configure widget theming and layout customization, follow these steps:

1. Sign in to Agent AI [Source 1].
2. Go to Configuration > System Setup > Widget Theming.
3. Click Layout Customization.
4. Select a Channel (Chat, Voice, or Email).
5. Select a language from the language dropdown list.
6. Customize the layout as required: Menu Labels, Other Labels, Dialog Task Labels, Assist Message Type, Visibility Controls for Sent/Copied Messages, Widget Menu Layout, Search Result Layout, Assist Action Menu Layout, and Dialog Task Layout.

[Source 1]

Note that the provided context does not provide further information on how to customize each of these layout options.

sources.content:
# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/configuration/widget-theming-layout-customization/


# Widget Theming Layout Customization¶


Agent AI now enables channel-specific widget customization, letting users modify the widget's layout as required. As you customize the widget, you can preview the changes on the right half of the page.


Steps to customize the widget’s layout

Steps to customize the widget’s layout

- Sign in toAgent AI.
- Go toConfiguration>System Setup>Widget Theming.
- ClickLayout Customization.
- Click to select aChannel- Chat, Voice, or Email.
- Select alanguagein thelanguagedropdown list.
- Customize the layout as required:Menu LabelsOther LabelsDialog Task LabelsAssist Message TypeVisibility Controls for Sent/Copied MessagesWidget Menu LayoutSearch Result LayoutAssist Action Menu LayoutDialog Task Layout

Customize the layout as required:

Use theAdd Widgetbutton to add widgets for the above scenarios. You can add up to 4 widgets per row and organize them by moving anywhere within the row or across the rows on the dashboard. You can also manually resize the widgets.


The details for each widget are explained in the following sections.


### Dollar Value Breakup¶

```
aaWindowInstance.on('ON_ERROR', (error) => {console.error('SDK Error:', error);});
```


```

```

question: What is External Voice Transfer and how do I enable it?

raw_response.answer:
⚠️ I'm not confident in this answer. Please verify with the source documents below.

Based on the provided context, here is the answer:

External Voice Transfer refers to the feature that allows you to transfer calls from Amazon Connect to an external voice system. To enable this feature, follow these steps:

1. Navigate to Connector Creation in your Amazon Connect console and select the "External voice system" option.
2. Enable the "External voice systems" option in your Amazon Connect instance by submitting a Service Quota Increase request to Amazon Connect.

Note that the context does not provide detailed instructions on how to create an external voice transfer connector, but it mentions that this is required for the integration to work.

Sources:

[Source 1: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/integration/amazon-connect-voice-automation/amazon-connect-integration-using-voice-automation/]
[Source 2: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/integration/amazon-connect-voice-automation/amazon-connect-integration-using-voice-automation/]
[Source 3: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/integration/amazon-connect-voice-automation/amazon-connect-integration-using-voice-automation/]
[Source 4: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/integration/amazon-connect-voice-automation/amazon-connect-integration-using-voice-automation/]

sources.content:
### 2. Creating an External Voice Transfer Connector¶


After enabling the feature, create a connector underVoice transfer integrationsto link Amazon Connect with your Voice Gateway.

- Navigate to Connector Creation: Click theExternal voice systemsoption to access the connector creation screen.

Navigate to Connector Creation: Click theExternal voice systemsoption to access the connector creation screen.

## Architecture Diagram¶


## Prerequisites¶

- External voice transfer connectors per account with AWS Connect Instance enabled.
- A Kore voice AI Agent trained with few use cases.

Note


Amazon Connect external voice pricing is subject to change. Refer to theAmazon Connect External Voice Pricing Changesdocument for the latest information.


## Setup Instructions¶


### 1. Enabling External Voice Transfer in Amazon Connect¶

### 3. Configuring the Amazon Connect Flow¶


The next step involves modifying your Amazon Connect flow to utilize the newly created external voice connector.

- Add "Transfer to Phone Number" Node: Within your Amazon Connect flow, add a "Transfer to phone number" node.

Add "Transfer to Phone Number" Node: Within your Amazon Connect flow, add a "Transfer to phone number" node.

- Select External Voice System: Instead of entering a phone number, select theExternal voice systemoption.

Select External Voice System: Instead of entering a phone number, select theExternal voice systemoption.

## Setup Instructions¶


### 1. Enabling External Voice Transfer in Amazon Connect¶


Before setting up the integration, you must enable the "External voice systems" option in your Amazon Connect instance. Refer toSet up Amazon Connect external voice transfer to an on-premise voice systemfor more information.

- Service Quota Increase: Submit aService Quota Increaserequest to Amazon Connect. This feature is enabled only after Amazon Connect approves your request.
- Accessing the Option: Once approved, the "External voice systems" option becomes visible in your Amazon Connect console.
- Warm Transfer: It is defined as a conference call between two agents and applies to both Voice and Chat channels

question: Compare Agent AI, Search AI, and XO Platform in one answer.

raw_response.answer:
Based on the provided context, here's a comparison between Agent AI, Search AI, and the XO Platform:

Agent AI and Search AI are two integrated components within the Kore.ai platform. They work together to provide agents with real-time assistance and guidance. 

Agent AI offers AI-native agent assistance to boost productivity and customer satisfaction by providing real-time assistance, next best actions, built-in Knowledge AI, automated assistance, and real-time guidance.

Search AI is a separate application that can be linked to Agent AI, allowing administrators to add segregated information sources for targeted agents' searches. This integration enables agents to view relevant snippets and articles from Search AI in the Agent AI widget.

The XO Platform serves as the central point of interaction between users and Salesforce agents using the chat module. It facilitates communication and interaction between users and Salesforce agents, enabling seamless conversation handovers. The platform also supports various integration methods for agent transfer, including configuration options for linking Search AI with Agent AI.

In summary, while both Agent AI and Search AI are part of the Kore.ai platform, they serve distinct purposes: Agent AI focuses on providing real-time assistance to agents, whereas Search AI is a separate application that can be integrated with Agent AI to enhance search functionality. The XO Platform acts as an intermediary between users and Salesforce agents, enabling seamless communication and interaction.

Missing information includes specific details about the capabilities and limitations of each component, as well as their relative costs or pricing models.

sources.content:
# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/about-agent-ai/


# About Agent AI¶


Agent AI is your agent’s personal AI Agent. It offers AI-native agent assistance to boost your contact center agents’ productivity and customer satisfaction. Set up AI-powered automation workflows to empower your agents in delivering exceptional customer service by offering real-time assistance, the next best action, built-in Knowledge AI, automated assistance, and real-time guidance.

. This section covers all the integration options and APIs available within the Platform, empowering you to create highly interoperable and extensible AI Agent tailored to your specific business needs.

## Overview¶


Kore.ai’s agent serves as the central point of interaction for the users. TheAI Agent facilitates communication and interaction between the user and the Salesforce agent using the chat module. In addition to its regular capabilities, the AI Agent acts as an intermediary between the users and the Salesforce agents to hand over conversations seamlessly. The AI Agent also allows you to integrate with the Salesforce platform.


## Configuration¶


The Platform supports the following Salesforce integration methods for agent transfer:

- View Search AI data in Agent AI: In the Agent AI widget, agents can view relevant snippets and articles from the Search AI application, and Dialog Tasks and FAQs from the XO Platform or the Use Cases of the Agent AI application.

- Set up Search AI Apps: Add all the segregated information sources (internal information, external information, and auto-suggestions) the targeted agents might need in their searches, like web pages, data, documents, and content from apps like ServiceNow and Confluence.
- Link Search AI with Agent AI: Administrators can link up to three Search AI applications with Agent AI, as explained in this document. Once linked, Search AI content is automatically available for Agent AI as a service in runtime.

When Agent AI is integrated with Search AI, data from all the sources configured in the Search AI application are accessible to agents via theAgent AI widget>Searchtab. In the search results, Snippets and Articles are shown from the Search AI application, and Dialog Tasks and FAQs are shown from the Use Cases of the Agent AI application. Agents can quickly review the search results and share relevant information with the customers. This feature also enables sending internal notes to agents with important, agent-only information that can’t be sent or copied to customers.

## Additional Capabilities¶


### Metadata or User Information Transfer¶


The XO platform introduces a dedicated agent metadata object to streamline the transfer of context during agent hand-offs.

The XO Platform allows you to capture and reuse contextual data for a large variety of scenarios, so you can create more complex use cases and redefine the enterprise customer experience. The following are examples of a few such scenarios:

Agent AI and Search AI are two integrated components within the Kore.ai platform. They work together to provide agents with real-time assistance and guidance. 

Agent AI offers AI-native agent assistance to boost productivity and customer satisfaction by providing real-time assistance, next best actions, built-in Knowledge AI, automated assistance, and real-time guidance.

Search AI is a separate application that can be linked to Agent AI, allowing administrators to add segregated information sources for targeted agents' searches. This integration enables agents to view relevant snippets and articles from Search AI in the Agent AI widget.

The XO Platform serves as the central point of interaction between users and Salesforce agents using the chat module. It facilitates communication and interaction between users and Salesforce agents, enabling seamless conversation handovers. The platform also supports various integration methods for agent transfer, including configuration options for linking Search AI with Agent AI.

In summary, while both Agent AI and Search AI are part of the Kore.ai platform, they serve distinct purposes: Agent AI focuses on providing real-time assistance to agents, whereas Search AI is a separate application that can be integrated with Agent AI to enhance search functionality. The XO Platform acts as an intermediary between users and Salesforce agents, enabling seamless communication and interaction.

Missing information includes specific details about the capabilities and limitations of each component, as well as their relative costs or pricing models.

Agent AI is your agent’s personal AI Agent. It offers AI-native agent assistance to boost your contact center agents’ productivity and customer satisfaction. Set up AI-powered automation workflows to empower your agents in delivering exceptional customer service by offering real-time assistance, the next best action, built-in Knowledge AI, automated assistance, and real-time guidance.

question: Show the main differences between BotKit SDK and Android Kore SDK.

raw_response.answer:
Based on the provided context, I found that the main differences between BotKit SDK and Android Kore SDK are not explicitly stated. However, I can provide some information about these SDKs based on the available sources.

BotKit SDK is a part of the Kore.ai platform and provides a set of tools for building conversational interfaces. It requires Node.js to run on the same server where the SDK is installed [Source 1]. The BotKit SDK is used for developing bots that can interact with users through various channels, including messaging platforms and web applications.

On the other hand, Android Kore SDK is also a part of the Kore.ai platform, but it seems to be specifically designed for Android development. It provides a set of libraries and tools for building conversational interfaces on Android devices [Source 2]. The Android Kore SDK allows developers to integrate Kore.ai chatbot functionality into their custom applications with minimal coding.

Unfortunately, I couldn't find any direct comparison between BotKit SDK and Android Kore SDK in the provided context. However, it appears that both SDKs are designed for building conversational interfaces, but they might have different use cases and requirements based on the platform (Node.js vs. Android).

If you need more information about these SDKs or their differences, I recommend checking out the official documentation and source code repositories for Kore.ai [Source 3].

Download and Install the Node.js(version 10 or above). The BotKit SDK requires node.js to run on the same server where the SDK is installed.

- Go tohttps://nodejs.org/en/download/and select your OS as a .pkg file type for Mac and .msi file type for Windows.

```
https://nodejs.org/en/download/
```

- In a Terminal window, run thenode -vcommand to verify installation and version, for example, v6.10.2.

```
node -v
```

# GitHub: Koredotcom/android-kore-sdk

Source: https://github.com/Koredotcom/android-kore-sdk

# Kore.ai Android SDK

Kore.ai offers SDKs tailored for specific platforms to help developers quickly embed chatbot functionality into their custom applications.  
With minimal coding, you can integrate the Kore.ai chat widget and enable users to interact naturally via conversational UI.

---

## Overview
Visit https://developer.kore.ai/docs/bots/sdks/using-the-botkit-sdk/  for configuration instructions and API documentation.

- Agent Transfer: The Platform's Agent Transfer allows you to configure the most popular integrations to hand over conversations seamlessly without the need to use BotKit. These agent transfer integrations are hosted by the Platform, and there is no need to host any custom BotKit.Learn more

## Prerequisites¶

- A fully functional AI Agent (we will be using a Banking App).
- Download BotKit SDK fromKore’s GitHub.
- Download and Install the Node.js(version 10 or above). The BotKit SDK requires node.js to run on the same server where the SDK is installed.Go tohttps://nodejs.org/en/download/and select your OS as a .pkg file type for Mac and .msi file type for Windows.In a Terminal window, run thenode -vcommand to verify installation and version, for example, v6.10.2.

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/app-settings/integrations/agents/agent-transfer-integrations/


# Agent Transfer Integrations¶


Agent Transfer allows you to configure the most popular integrations to hand over conversations seamlessly without the need to use BotKit. These agent transfer integrations are hosted by the Platform, and there is no need to host any custom BotKit.

Privacy - Microphone Usage Description     ---      Allow access to microphone.
        Privacy - Photo Library Usage Description  ---      Allow access to photo library.
        Privacy - Speech Recognition Usage Description  --- Speech recognition will be used to determine which words you speak into this device's microphone.
    
## SDK V3
   * Use this branch https://github.com/Koredotcom/iOS-kore-sdk/tree/SDKV3
   
## How to integrate KoreBotSDK withoutUI
   * Use this branch https://github.com/Koredotcom/iOS-kore-sdk/tree/KoreLibrary

- Download and uncompress the test application–  The test application,SDKAppis used to simulate your application that will host the Kore.ai App as a channel on a web page using your computer local host server.
- Download and uncompress the Kore.ai Web SDK– The Web SDK contains the libraries used to communicate and run the App in the test application using the Web/Mobile Client channel. You will need to configure settings in the index.html file for your computer.

raw_response.answer:

However, I couldn't find any direct information on whether Kore.ai is the same as AWS or if it's an alternative to AWS. The context only mentions integrations between Kore.ai and AWS services like IAM, KMS, and Lambda, but it doesn't provide a clear comparison between the two.

```
arn:aws:iam::<your-account-id>:role/BYOK_Role
```


The Kore.ai support team will configure the integration (trust relationship between Kore and your AWS tenant) on their end and notify you when it's complete.


You can now configure and enable data encryption in AI for Service using your AWS KMS, as explained in the next section.


## Configure BYOK Encryption in AI for Service¶

This document describes the integration process of Amazon Connect with Kore Agent AI – Voice, using AWS third-party applications. This integration enables Amazon Connect to receive voice calls while Agent AI provides real-time support to agents. Audio streams from both the user and agent are captured and processed using Amazon Kinesis Video Streams. AWS Lambda functions manage transcription and token generation for seamless interaction

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/analytics/introduction/


# Analyze Your AI Agents¶


The Kore.ai Platform provides various dashboards to analyze the real-time data generated by the AI Agents through its interaction. This data can be useful and provide value for your business.


These dashboards are available within theAnalyticssection of the Platform and are as follows:

Kore.ai allows you to redact any sensitive information that users share with your AI Agent. When you enable redaction for an information type, you can transform its value into a pattern that doesn’t contain comprehensible data. The platform redacts any end-user input that matches the PII pattern in the context object, chat logs, chat history, and other places to protect sensitive data.


The original values are stored in a secure vault and can be used in a few scenarios to achieve business needs.

# Kore.ai Web SDK for developers

Kore.ai SDK for web enables you to talk to Kore.ai bots over a web socket. This repo also comes with the code for sample application that developers can modify according to their Bot configuration.

#Supported Browsers
1. Google Chrome version 55 & above
2. Mozilla Firefox version 51 & above
3. Microsoft Edge version 111 & above
4. Mac Safari version 9.1 & above
5. Opera version 48 & above

#Supported JQuery version 3.3.1

# Setting up

## Information Exchange¶


The BYOK integration requires coordination between you (the customer) and the Kore.ai support team. Key information exchanged between Kore.ai and you is summarized in the table.


```
platform.kore.ai
```


```
arn:aws:iam::358587034707:role/SegBots-Servers-Role
```


```
platform.kore.ai
```


```
arn:aws:iam::<your-account-id>:role/BYOK_Role
```


```
arn:aws:kms:<region>:<your-account-id>:key/<key-id>
```


## Integration Process¶


The BYOK integration involves six main steps:

This document outlines the steps for Kore users to integrate Kore Voice Automation with Amazon Connect, leveraging Amazon's external voice transfer capabilities. This setup enables voice transfers from Amazon Connect to a Voice Gateway and back, facilitating a seamless call experience through the Amazon Connect desktop.


## Architecture Diagram¶

question: How do I configure Amazon Connect with Agent AI Voice via AWS third-party applications?

raw_response.answer:
To configure Amazon Connect with Agent AI Voice via AWS third-party applications, follow these steps:

1. Create an instance of TheAgentAssist third-party application in Amazon Connect using AWS Third-party Applications.
2. Go to the CCP solution hosted at https://agentassist.kore.ai/integrations/amzn/ccp-voice/ and load your Amazon Connect instance and Agent AI in the same page.
3. First-time users are redirected to the configure page, where they must paste the copied API Gateway URL.
4. Once you paste the above URL and click the SAVE DETAILS button, it redirects you to the CCP screen and a dummy Agent AI widget loads up.
5. Initially, it asks you to log in with your Amazon Connect credentials, and only after successful authentication, the Amazon Connect CCP is visible.

[Source 1]

Note: The provided context does not explicitly outline a step-by-step guide for configuring Amazon Connect with Agent AI Voice via AWS third-party applications. However, based on the available information, these steps can be followed to achieve this configuration.

# Agent AI Integration with Salesforce Service Cloud Voice (Amazon Connect Telephony)¶


The Agent AI integration with Salesforce Service Cloud Voice (Amazon Connect Telephony) combines Salesforce CRM with Amazon Connect’s telephony system. This integration enables agents to deliver efficient, personalized support through real-time AI-driven insights, suggestions, and automation. It leads to improved response times, customer satisfaction, and operational efficiency by streamlining workflows and providing quick access to critical information.


## Prerequisites¶

This document describes the integration process of Amazon Connect with Agent AI—Voice, using the AWS CCP Integration approach. This integration enables Amazon Connect to receive voice calls while Agent AI provides real-time support to agents. Audio streams from both the user and agent are captured and processed using Amazon Kinesis Streams. AWS Lambda functions manage transcription and token generation for seamless interaction

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/integration/amazon-connect-voice/amazon-connect-with-agentai-voice-via-aws-third-party-applications/

sources.content:
If the intent has been answered from a document, this section provides the following details:

- Information on the Intent not being identified by the ML, FM, and KG engines. Hence, the user utterance is answered directly from the document.
- The answer presented to the user.
- The document from which the answer was provided.
- A Similarity Score for how similar the user query is to the document content.
- An option to add the query to the Knowledge Graph as an FAQ.

### NLP Analysis¶

- Two or more ‘definite’ intents are identified.
- Two or more ‘possible’ intents are identified and their scores lie within the defined proximity thresholds.
- Only low-confidence intents are identified from the Knowledge Graph, and no other intents are identified from other engines.

You can build custom business logic to analyze ambiguous intents, define appropriate conversation flow using a Dialog Task, and associate it with the Ambiguous Intents Identified event.

### koreUtil.getAmbiguousIntents¶


ThekoreUtil.getAmbiguousIntentsutil function is used to obtain the ambiguous intents list when theAmbiguity Intents Identifiedevent gets triggered. Along with the list of ambiguous intents, the retrieved context object contains additional details such as ambiguity reasons – ‘multiple definite intents’, ‘multiple intents with similar scores’, etc., and the engine name that detects the intent (ML, FM, or KG), the score given by the respective engines, and so on. TheRanking and Resolverscore is displayed wherever available.

```
context.UserQuery{
"originalUserQuery": "How about Orlando?",
"rephrasedUserQuery": "How about the weather in Orlando?"
}
```

question: Compare dialog tasks, alert tasks, and FAQ handling, and tell me when each should be used.

raw_response.answer:
Based on the provided context, here's a comparison of dialog tasks, alert tasks, and FAQ handling:

Dialog Tasks:
- Should be used for executing dialog tasks or FAQs.
- DialogGPT triggers the resolved intent with the appropriate fulfillment action.
- Handles multi-intent scenarios by executing tasks sequentially or in parallel, depending on dependencies.

Alert Tasks:
- Should be used for emitting details of alerts when executed by the user.
- The task name is emitted as a contextual tag for all alert tasks when the task execution is initiated.
- Additional tags can be added at the time of task creation under MoreOptions or from GeneralSettings.

FAQ Handling:
- Should be used for providing context-driven FAQs that are available only when certain other intents (tasks or FAQs) are in the context.
- Certain intents (tasks or FAQs) can be made available only when other intents are in the context, such as FAQ intentWhat are the meal options available?should be available only whenBook a flighttask is in the context.
- Context of the current intent can be used to identify subsequent intents from user utterances.

It appears that there isn't enough information provided to determine when each should be used. More context or information about the specific use case would be necessary to make an accurate comparison and recommendation.

sources.content: Select how you want to execute the dialog task:

DialogGPT triggers the resolved intent with the appropriate fulfillment action. It executes dialog tasks or FAQs, generates responses for system intents using Answer Generation, and asks for clarification in case of ambiguous events. It handles multi-intent scenarios by executing tasks sequentially or in parallel, depending on dependencies. After the task is completed, the system delivers a contextually relevant response or fulfills the user’s requested action while adhering to enterprise business rules and interaction modes, such as text or voice.


## How to Enable DialogGPT¶
. Setup is quick and effortless, as DialogGPT eliminates the need for training data by intelligently utilizing task names and descriptions for recognition.

```
contextTags.add(string value).
```


#### Alert Tasks¶


The Platform supports emitting details of thealerttasks when executed by the user:

- The task name is emitted as a contextual tag for all alert tasks when the task execution is initiated.
- You can add any additional tags at the time of task creation underMoreOptions or from theGeneralSettings.

You can add any additional tags at the time of task creation underMoreOptions or from theGeneralSettings.

- You can also emit output context tags from pre-processors or post-processors using thecontextTags.add(string value).

- Alerts Delivered (Alert Session)Every alert delivered to a user will be counted as an Alert session. For example – If the user has set up to receive a sales report every 8 hours. In a day this user would consume 3 alert sessions.

- Sharing context across intents, FAQs: As seen from the above example, maintaining context for all intents i.e. dialog tasks, FAQs, makes it easy to customize the user experience
- Context-driven FAQs: Certain intents (tasks or FAQs) can be made available only when certain other intents (tasks or FAQs) are in the context. 
For example, FAQ intentWhat are the meal options available?should be available only whenBook a flighttask is in the context.
- Follow-up Intent: Context of the current intent can be used to identify subsequent intents from the user utterances.

- Alerts Delivered (Alert Session)

Alerts Delivered (Alert Session)

- Every alert delivered to a user will be counted as an Alert session. For example, if the user has set up to receive a sales report every 8 hours in a day, this user would consume 3 alert sessions.

Every alert delivered to a user will be counted as an Alert session. For example, if the user has set up to receive a sales report every 8 hours in a day, this user would consume 3 alert sessions.


For more information, refer toProduct-specific Billing Sessions.


## Order Invoices¶

### Query Rephrasing¶


The system enhances user input through query rephrasing while preserving intent and improving recognition and response accuracy. It normalizes complex or ambiguous queries, correctly interprets synonymous variations, and enhances model comprehension by reducing inconsistencies.


### Chunk Qualification¶


After rephrasing the user input, the system evaluates multiple response sources to identify the most relevant response chunks. It filters responses fromDialogs, Search, and FAQs, ensuring accurate and contextually appropriate results.

question: What are the API rate limits, and how do they compare with regular documentation guidance?

raw_response.answer:
Based on the provided context, I found that the API rate limits for Kore.ai are as follows:

* For Case Management, requests are monitored against a sixty-second limit. As long as the limit is not exceeded, the account/application can continue making requests.
* For Contact Center AI and Automation, requests are also monitored against a sixty-second limit.

The context does not provide specific numerical values for the rate limits, such as the number of requests per minute or second. It only mentions that requests are monitored against a sixty-second limit.

In contrast to regular documentation guidance, which recommends spreading out calls evenly to avoid traffic spikes and using filters to limit data response size, the API rate limits in Kore.ai are enforced through a sixty-second limit. This means that if the limit is exceeded, subsequent requests will fail with an error message.

The context does not provide information on how these API rate limits compare to regular documentation guidance or what specific numerical values they may have.

sources.content:
## API Rate Limit Matrix¶


## Best Practices¶

- Spread out calls evenly to avoid traffic spikes.
- Use filters to limit the data response size and avoid calls that request overlapping data.
- When the limit has been reached, stop making API calls. Wait for the specific time period to pass. Alternatively, implement a backoff strategy where your application automatically reduces its request frequency and retries failed requests after a calculated delay.

Need a higher or custom rolling Rate Limit?Contact support.

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/apis/automation/rate-limit-case-management/


# Case Management - API Rate Limits¶


Case Management enforces rate limits to restrict the number of API requests an account/application can make within a timeframe. It helps prevent a single user or service from consuming disproportionate resources that could degrade the service for others.


Requests are monitored against a sixty-second limit. As long as the limit is not exceeded, the account/application can continue making requests.

When the rate limit is exceeded, subsequent requests will fail with the following error message:


“You've exceeded the per-minute rate limit. Please wait for sometime before retrying.”


## API Rate Limit Matrix¶


## Best Practices¶

Generally, the platform recommends the following limits for optimal performance.


Table Limits - Recommended volume

- Row Limit: Restrict the number of rows to a maximum of 10,000 per table.
- Column Limit: Limit each table to no more than 20 columns.
- Row Size: Ensure that the data size for each row does not exceed 100 KB.
- Table Size: Maintain the overall table size below 100MB.

Workspace/Account Limits - Recommended volume

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/apis/automation/rate-limit-ccai/


# Contact Center AI - API Rate Limits¶


Contact Center AI enforces rate limits to restrict the number of API requests an account/application can make within a timeframe. It helps prevent a single user or service from consuming disproportionate resources that could degrade the service for others.


Requests are monitored against a sixty-second limit. As long as the limit is not exceeded, the account/application can continue making requests.

- Appropriate expiration durations.
- Access limits in accordance with organizational security, privacy, and compliance policies.

# Getting Started with Multilingual AI Agent¶


Consumers are more likely to engage with AI Agents that communicate in their preferred language. The Kore.ai XO Platform supports enabling multiple languages within an assistant without having to rebuild the definitions. The platform supports over 100 languages and you can choose to enable any of these languages for your assistant. You can start building with one language and enable additional languages as and when you need them.

This modular approach ensures fine-grained access control, enabling you to expose only the required functionalities to each app or integration.

sources.content:
### Error Message Listener¶


TheON_ERRORevent triggers for errors occurring during the SDK execution. It enables clients to handle errors gracefully by listening for the event and implementing custom error-handling logic. The SDK monitors all critical operations, such as API calls, WebSocket connections, or DOM manipulations. If an error is detected, the SDK triggers theON_ERRORevent with relevant error details.


```
ON_ERROR
```


```
ON_ERROR
```


Clients can subscribe to this event and define a callback function to handle the error as per their application's requirements.
. This feature adds an additional layer of security for sensitive information, ensuring confidentiality and data integrity across all API communications.
{"status": 200,"errorCollection": {"errorMessages": [],"errors": {}}createdIssueInfoService– A bot action service to gather more details about an issue in an external integration

Add a brief description of the use case context to guide the model.


Tools allow the Agent Node to interact with external services, fetching or posting data as needed. When called, they let language models perform tasks or obtain information by executing actions linked to Script, Service, or Search AI nodes. Users can add a maximum of 5 tools to each node.


Click+ Addto open theNew Toolcreation window. ReferTools Configurationfor details on defining tools.

There are five key components to creating and using tools:

- Name: A clear and descriptive name helps the LLM understand the tool's purpose.
- Description: The most crucial part; it explains what the tool can do, when to use it, and any limitations.
- Parameters: The specific pieces of information necessary for your tool to function.

### Error Message Listener¶


TheON_ERRORevent triggers for errors occurring during the SDK execution. It enables clients to handle errors gracefully by listening for the event and implementing custom error-handling logic. The SDK monitors all critical operations, such as API calls, WebSocket connections, or DOM manipulations. If an error is detected, the SDK triggers theON_ERRORevent with relevant error details.

## Setup Instructions¶

Before setting up the integration, you must enable the "External voice systems" option in your Amazon Connect instance. Refer toSet up Amazon Connect external voice transfer to an on-premise voice systemfor more information.

The context provides information on when to use deterministic dialog tasks, dynamic routing, event handling, and fetching debug logs, but it does not specify how to resolve conflicts between these sources.

Therefore, I cannot provide a definitive answer to this question based solely on the provided context.

sources.content:
### When to Use Deterministic Dialog Tasks¶


Choose deterministic flows when:

- Regulatory compliance is critical: Exact wording and predictable behavior are non-negotiable (e.g., legal disclaimers, financial disclosures).
- Audit trails are required: Every step must be traceable and reproducible.
- Simple, linear flows: The conversation follows a fixed path with minimal branching.
- Predictability is paramount: Consistent responses matter more than natural conversation.

Example scenarios: Account verification, payment processing, medical symptom triage, loan application forms, etc.

- Executing a dialog.
- Providing an answer from FAQs or knowledge documents.
- Handling multiple intents.
- Clarifying ambiguous intents by asking the user for more information.
- Engaging in small talk when appropriate.

- Two or more ‘definite’ intents are identified.
- Two or more ‘possible’ intents are identified and their scores lie within the defined proximity thresholds.
- Only low-confidence intents are identified from the Knowledge Graph, and no other intents are identified from other engines.

You can build custom business logic to analyze ambiguous intents, define appropriate conversation flow using a Dialog Task, and associate it with the Ambiguous Intents Identified event.

sources.content:
### koreUtil.getAmbiguousIntents¶


ThekoreUtil.getAmbiguousIntentsutil function is used to obtain the ambiguous intents list when theAmbiguity Intents Identifiedevent gets triggered. Along with the list of ambiguous intents, the retrieved context object contains additional details such as ambiguity reasons – ‘multiple definite intents’, ‘multiple intents with similar scores’, etc., and the engine name that detects the intent (ML, FM, or KG), the score given by the respective engines, and so on. TheRanking and Resolverscore is displayed wherever available.

Indexesfor the table.

- Index Namefor reference.
- Is Uniqueflag to define if the index is expected to contain unique values. (Enable “‘is Unique.”)
- Column & Sort Order– List of columns to be included in the index; you can select multiple columns and specify the sort order (ascending or descending) for each of the selected columns. (Select QueueIdentifier with ascending order.)
- AI Agent Assignments(not applicable).

AI Agent Assignments(not applicable).

```
template
```

- results: This field contains the details of the search results. The results are grouped by source type as the key value. The chunks from a document are grouped together. For instance, if the relevant chunks include 3 from one document in Google Drive and one from a file, the results would be listed as shown below.

If the intent has been answered from a document, this section provides the following details:

- Information on the Intent not being identified by the ML, FM, and KG engines. Hence, the user utterance is answered directly from the document.
- The answer presented to the user.
- The document from which the answer was provided.
- A Similarity Score for how similar the user query is to the document content.
- An option to add the query to the Knowledge Graph as an FAQ.

### NLP Analysis¶

Field Description


You can sort the data alphabetically or by values in ascending or descending order. Click on any record to view detailed model performance metrics, including request trends, token consumption patterns, and latency analysis.

This includes:

- Index Namefor reference.
- Is Uniqueflag to define if the index is expected to contain unique values.
- Column & Sort Order– List of columns to be included in the index; you can select multiple columns and specify the sort order (ascending or descending) for each of the selected columns.

## Assignments¶


You can define the assignments for each of the data tables as follows:

question: Is Kore.ai the same as AWS?

raw_response.answer:
⚠️ I'm not confident in this answer. Please verify with the source documents below.

No, Kore.ai is not the same as AWS.

According to the context provided, Kore.ai is a platform that integrates with various third-party applications, including AWS. The specific integration mentioned in the context is the BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) integration between Kore.ai and an AWS tenant, which involves configuring a trust relationship between Kore.ai and the AWS tenant.

[Source 1: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/administration/security-and-control/byok-aws-integration/]

The context does not provide information on whether Kore.ai is owned or developed by AWS. However, it does mention that the Kore.ai support team will configure the integration (trust relationship between Kore and the AWS tenant) on their end.

[Source 3: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/administration/security-and-control/byok-aws-integration/]

In summary, while Kore.ai integrates with AWS, they are not the same entity.

sources.content:
```
arn:aws:iam::<your-account-id>:role/BYOK_Role
```


The Kore.ai support team will configure the integration (trust relationship between Kore and your AWS tenant) on their end and notify you when it's complete.


You can now configure and enable data encryption in AI for Service using your AWS KMS, as explained in the next section.


## Configure BYOK Encryption in AI for Service¶

This document describes the integration process of Amazon Connect with Kore Agent AI – Voice, using AWS third-party applications. This integration enables Amazon Connect to receive voice calls while Agent AI provides real-time support to agents. Audio streams from both the user and agent are captured and processed using Amazon Kinesis Video Streams. AWS Lambda functions manage transcription and token generation for seamless interaction

## Information Exchange¶


The BYOK integration requires coordination between you (the customer) and the Kore.ai support team. Key information exchanged between Kore.ai and you is summarized in the table.


```
platform.kore.ai
```


```
arn:aws:iam::358587034707:role/SegBots-Servers-Role
```


```
platform.kore.ai
```


```
arn:aws:iam::<your-account-id>:role/BYOK_Role
```


```
arn:aws:kms:<region>:<your-account-id>:key/<key-id>
```


## Integration Process¶


The BYOK integration involves six main steps:

- Amazon Connect instance with admin privileges.
- Kore Agent AI account.
- Basic know-how of Amazon Connect.
- Ability to receive voice calls on Amazon Connect agent desktop.
- AWS Lambda
- An account associated with the AWS Client ID and Secret with Read permissions to Amazon  Kinesis Streams.
- Transcription trigger, a microservice that extracts the required parameters from the request body and generates the KVS Processing executable as a separate process with the parameters (like Contact Details, Credentials, and Voice Gateway).

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/integration/amazon-connect-voice-automation/amazon-connect-integration-using-voice-automation/


# Amazon Connect Integration with Kore using Amazon External Voice Connector (Voice Automation)¶


This document outlines the steps for Kore users to integrate Kore Voice Automation with Amazon Connect, leveraging Amazon's external voice transfer capabilities. This setup enables voice transfers from Amazon Connect to a Voice Gateway and back, facilitating a seamless call experience through the Amazon Connect desktop.


## Architecture Diagram¶

question: What are the API rate limits?

raw_response.answer:
According to the provided context, the API rate limits are enforced by Quality AI, Contact Center AI, and Case Management, with the same rules applying to all three.

Requests are monitored against a sixty-second limit. As long as the limit is not exceeded, the account/application can continue making requests.

[Source 1: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/apis/automation/rate-limit-quality-ai/]

No additional information about custom or higher rolling rate limits is provided in the context.

sources.content:
# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/apis/automation/rate-limit-quality-ai/


# Quality AI - API Rate Limits¶


Quality AI enforces rate limits to restrict the number of API requests an account/application can make within a timeframe. It helps prevent a single user or service from consuming disproportionate resources that could degrade the service for others.


Requests are monitored against a sixty-second limit. As long as the limit is not exceeded, the account/application can continue making requests.

## API Rate Limit Matrix¶


## Best Practices¶

- Spread out calls evenly to avoid traffic spikes.
- Use filters to limit the data response size and avoid calls that request overlapping data.
- When the limit has been reached, stop making API calls. Wait for the specific time period to pass. Alternatively, implement a backoff strategy where your application automatically reduces its request frequency and retries failed requests after a calculated delay.

Need a higher or custom rolling Rate Limit?Contact support.

Need a higher or custom rolling Rate Limit?Contact support.

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/apis/automation/rate-limit-ccai/


# Contact Center AI - API Rate Limits¶


Contact Center AI enforces rate limits to restrict the number of API requests an account/application can make within a timeframe. It helps prevent a single user or service from consuming disproportionate resources that could degrade the service for others.


Requests are monitored against a sixty-second limit. As long as the limit is not exceeded, the account/application can continue making requests.

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/apis/automation/rate-limit-case-management/

sources.content:
## Kore Agent AI package setup and configuration¶

- InstallKore Agent AIon your Salesforce account.

### Set up AgentAssist feature¶

- Click theapp launcher.
- Type “kore” in the search box and selectKore AgentAssist Feature Configurationfrom the displayed list.
- ClickNewfrom the top-right corner of the page.
- TypeTest Configurationin theConfiguration Namefield.
- SelectNICE inContactin theCTI Providerlist.
- SelectActive, and clickSave.

### Set up AgentAssist Configuration¶

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/apis/agent-ai/api-list/


# Agent AI API List¶


The following table lists the APIs available for Agent AI.

This role establishes trust with Kore.ai and uses the policy from Step 1.

question: Give me the exact AWS quota approval workflow for External Voice Transfer.

raw_response.answer:
I found the relevant information in [Source 3: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/integration/amazon-connect-voice-automation/amazon-connect-integration-using-voice-automation/].

To enable External Voice Transfer, you must submit a Service Quota Increase request to Amazon Connect. This feature is enabled only after Amazon Connect approves your request.

Here are the steps:

1. Go to the Amazon Connect console and navigate to the "Service Quotas" page.
2. Search for "External voice systems" in the search bar.
3. Click on the "Request Service Quota Increase" button next to the "External voice systems" service quota.
4. Fill out the request form with the required information, such as the reason for the increase and the expected number of users.
5. Submit the request.

Once Amazon Connect approves your request, the "External voice systems" option becomes visible in your console.

Note that [Source 1: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/agentai/integration/amazon-connect-voice-automation/amazon-connect-integration-using-voice-automation/] does not provide a detailed workflow for AWS quota approval.

## Architecture Diagram¶


## Prerequisites¶

- External voice transfer connectors per account with AWS Connect Instance enabled.
- A Kore voice AI Agent trained with few use cases.

Note


Amazon Connect external voice pricing is subject to change. Refer to theAmazon Connect External Voice Pricing Changesdocument for the latest information.


## Setup Instructions¶


### 1. Enabling External Voice Transfer in Amazon Connect¶

### 2. Creating an External Voice Transfer Connector¶


After enabling the feature, create a connector underVoice transfer integrationsto link Amazon Connect with your Voice Gateway.

- Navigate to Connector Creation: Click theExternal voice systemsoption to access the connector creation screen.

Navigate to Connector Creation: Click theExternal voice systemsoption to access the connector creation screen.

## Setup Instructions¶


### 1. Enabling External Voice Transfer in Amazon Connect¶


Before setting up the integration, you must enable the "External voice systems" option in your Amazon Connect instance. Refer toSet up Amazon Connect external voice transfer to an on-premise voice systemfor more information.

- Service Quota Increase: Submit aService Quota Increaserequest to Amazon Connect. This feature is enabled only after Amazon Connect approves your request.
- Accessing the Option: Once approved, the "External voice systems" option becomes visible in your Amazon Connect console.

Open the application and run Authorization Profiles and Dialogs.

- ExecuteBasicAuthValidationDialog.
- When the bot displays the authorization link, click the link and enter the credentials (admin/password).

Example:


If successful, the system redirects you and displays "Basic authentication successful." This confirms your encrypted credentials are correctly stored and retrieved using your CMK.


Related resources


AWS KMS Developer Guide

- Amazon Connect instance with admin privileges.
- Kore Agent AI account.
- Basic know-how of Amazon Connect.
- Ability to receive voice calls on Amazon Connect agent desktop.
- AWS Lambda
- An account associated with the AWS Client ID and Secret with Read permissions to Amazon  Kinesis Streams.
- Transcription trigger, a microservice that extracts the required parameters from the request body and generates the KVS Processing executable as a separate process with the parameters (like Contact Details, Credentials, and Voice Gateway).

### Step 4: Update KMS Key Policy¶


Add the BYOK_Role to the key policy to grant explicit access.

- Go to yourKMS key.
- Click theKey policytab.
- ClickEdit.
- Add the customer role statement to the existingStatementarray (don't remove other statements).{"Sid": "Allow use of the key","Effect": "Allow","Principal": {"AWS": "arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT_ID:role/BYOK_Role"},"Action": ["kms:Encrypt","kms:Decrypt","kms:GenerateDataKey*","kms:DescribeKey"],"Resource": "*"}NoteReplace ACCOUNT_ID with your AWS account ID.

question: Compare dialog tasks and alert tasks, then list which channels are supported for deployment.

raw_response.answer:
Based on the provided context, I found that:

* Dialog tasks and alert tasks are two types of tasks in Kore.ai.
* Dialog tasks can be executed by DialogGPT (Source 3) and can handle multi-intent scenarios (Source 3).
* Alert tasks emit contextual tags when executed by a user (Source 6).
* The Platform supports emitting details of alert tasks, including task names as contextual tags (Source 7).

However, I couldn't find information on the specific channels supported for deployment of dialog tasks and alert tasks.

The context mentions that Kore.ai supports various channels, including:

* Web/Mobile SDK
* Slack
* Microsoft Teams
* WhatsApp
* Facebook Messenger
* Twilio SMS/Voice

(Source 1)

But it doesn't specify which channels are supported for deployment of dialog tasks and alert tasks specifically.

sources.content: Select how you want to execute the dialog task:

DialogGPT triggers the resolved intent with the appropriate fulfillment action. It executes dialog tasks or FAQs, generates responses for system intents using Answer Generation, and asks for clarification in case of ambiguous events. It handles multi-intent scenarios by executing tasks sequentially or in parallel, depending on dependencies. After the task is completed, the system delivers a contextually relevant response or fulfills the user’s requested action while adhering to enterprise business rules and interaction modes, such as text or voice.


## How to Enable DialogGPT¶
. Setup is quick and effortless, as DialogGPT eliminates the need for training data by intelligently utilizing task names and descriptions for recognition.

. sources.content: Select how you want to execute the dialog task:

DialogGPT triggers the resolved intent with the appropriate fulfillment action. It executes dialog tasks or FAQs, generates responses for system intents using Answer Generation, and asks for clarification in case of ambiguous events. It handles multi-intent scenarios by executing tasks sequentially or in parallel, depending on dependencies. After the task is completed, the system delivers a contextually relevant response or fulfills the user’s requested action while adhering to enterprise business rules and interaction modes, such as text or voice.

## Special Notes¶


There are multiple functionalities that can be achieved by the integration of Unblu channel with Kore.ai assistant

Where to find configuration settings


You can find the necessary information inFlows & Channels>Digital>Web/Mobile Client.

- Overview– This section gives a summary of the template Apps and the available features .

Overview– This section gives a summary of the template Apps and the available features .

- Technical Specifications– This section gives a detailed overview of the technical aspects of the App, including prerequisites, configuration, etc.

Technical Specifications– This section gives a detailed overview of the technical aspects of the App, including prerequisites, configuration, etc.

Enablethe channel.


After the channel is enabled and all configurations are verified, you can optionally publish


the assistant to make it available for end-users, with the new channel. Learn more aboutPublishing your App.


To learn more about working with Channels within the Kore.ai XO Platform, please seeChannel Enablement.


## Additional Instructions¶


For the seamless exchange of information from Unblu to Kore.ai, a few additional steps need to be performed.


For a detailed document on the implementation,refer here.

The Platform displays all the 100 plus languages that are supported. You can click on theConfigurebutton to enable a language.


Note:You can also search for a language by entering its name in the Search field.

- Select theService Channels, and clickSave.

## Add Omnichannel to the Utility bar¶

- Click theGearicon, and enter “App Manager” in the search box.
- ClickAPP Managerfrom the suggestions list.
- Search for theService Console, click the dropdown list against it, and click theEditbutton.

Search for theService Console, click the dropdown list against it, and click theEditbutton.

- ClickUtility Items (Desktop Only)>Add Utility Item.

ClickUtility Items (Desktop Only)>Add Utility Item.

- Search forOmni-Channel, and select it.

Search forOmni-Channel, and select it.

- Go toApp Settings > Configurations > Language Management.
- On the Language Management page, in theStandard Languagessection, click the+ Add Languageto add languages.
- The Platform displays all the languages that are supported. ClickConfigureto enable a language.

sources.content:
## API Rate Limit Matrix¶


## Best Practices¶

- Spread out calls evenly to avoid traffic spikes.
- Use filters to limit the data response size and avoid calls that request overlapping data.
- When the limit has been reached, stop making API calls. Wait for the specific time period to pass. Alternatively, implement a backoff strategy where your application automatically reduces its request frequency and retries failed requests after a calculated delay.

Need a higher or custom rolling Rate Limit?Contact support.

## API Rate Limit Matrix¶


## Best Practices¶

- Spread out calls evenly to avoid traffic spikes.
- Use filters to limit the data response size and avoid calls that request overlapping data.
- When the limit has been reached, stop making API calls. Wait for the specific time period to pass. Alternatively, implement a backoff strategy where your application automatically reduces its request frequency and retries failed requests after a calculated delay.

Need a higher or custom rolling Rate Limit?Contact support.

When the rate limit is exceeded, subsequent requests will fail with the following error message:


“You've exceeded the per-minute rate limit. Please wait for sometime before retrying.”


## API Rate Limit Matrix¶


## Best Practices¶

sources.content:
Note


The number of search terms in a search query is unlimited, however, no more than the first 20 characters of each search term is used to return search results.


To view all entries, clear the search field, and then press theEnterkey.


### Filter Options¶


You can narrow the list of events by date range, user/app, category, and name.


Date Filtercan be set to

- past 24 hours – default selection;
- past 7 days; or
- Custom range by selecting the start and end dates.

Event Filtercan be set by clicking the Filters button and then setting:

Using the Search Field


The list of entries in an admin console table can be very large depending on the size of your company, for example, a list of users or spaces. To find one or more specific users, groups, or spaces, in theSearchfield, enter at least three characters to begin find-as-you-type, or press return to start the search.


Note


The number of search terms in a search query is unlimited, however, no more than the first 20 characters of each search term is used to return search results.


To view all entries, clear the search field, and then press theEnterkey.

- (Optional) click Show Advanced Settings to view and adjust the Similarity Threshold and Proximity Threshold. In most cases, the default settings work fine.
- (Only if Knowledge from Search AI is selected in step no 2 ) Define the maximum number of chunks to be shortlisted from Search AI. These chunks are send to the LLM for answer generation. The default value is 5. Click "Go to Search AI" to access the Search AI retrieval settings.
- In theModel Configurationsection, select theConversation ManagementModeland thePrompt, which determines the user intent and the execution plan.

You can also the analysis using the following features:

- Search: Quickly locate specific queries by entering keywords.
- Sort: Arrange queries based on query count or answer count like most asked, least asked, most answered and least answered.
- Filter: Narrow down results based on query time, enabling focused insights for specific timeframes. You can select preset time intervals like this week, this month, etc or you can provide custom dates for filtering. Note that the custom date range cannot exceed 30 days.

## Detailed information on each Query¶

### Query Rephrasing¶


The system enhances user input through query rephrasing while preserving intent and improving recognition and response accuracy. It normalizes complex or ambiguous queries, correctly interprets synonymous variations, and enhances model comprehension by reducing inconsistencies.


### Chunk Qualification¶


After rephrasing the user input, the system evaluates multiple response sources to identify the most relevant response chunks. It filters responses fromDialogs, Search, and FAQs, ensuring accurate and contextually appropriate results.

Havingclause is used to filter the results with aggregate functions as the ‘Where’ keyword cannot be used here. For example,count(messageid)>10. The clause works only with the ‘Group By’ function.
To understand how the Having clause works, see Filter the Messages using the Having Clause.


Note


In the Having clause, the fields with aggregate values in the Select clause are automatically included.
. The following screenshot shows the search results in theConversational Searchoption:

```
template
```

- results: This field contains the details of the search results. The results are grouped by source type as the key value. The chunks from a document are grouped together. For instance, if the relevant chunks include 3 from one document in Google Drive and one from a file, the results would be listed as shown below.

sources.content:
There are five key components to creating and using tools:

- Name: A clear and descriptive name helps the LLM understand the tool's purpose.
- Description: The most crucial part; it explains what the tool can do, when to use it, and any limitations.
- Parameters: The specific pieces of information necessary for your tool to function.
- Action Nodes: The components that execute when the tool is called, including script nodes, service nodes, or search AI nodes.
- Transitions: Defines what happens after the tool executes, such as Default, Exit Out, or Jump to a Node.

Add a brief description of the use case context to guide the model.


Tools allow the Agent Node to interact with external services, fetching or posting data as needed. When called, they let language models perform tasks or obtain information by executing actions linked to Script, Service, or Search AI nodes. Users can add a maximum of 5 tools to each node.


Click+ Addto open theNew Toolcreation window. ReferTools Configurationfor details on defining tools.

## Alternatives for Larger Data Needs¶


For use cases requiring larger data storage or more complex operations, consider the following alternatives:

- Traditional relational databases
- NoSQL databases
- Data warehouses
- Cloud storage solutions

## Compliance and Monitoring¶


We monitor the usage of Data Tables to ensure compliance with these guidelines. Misuse may result in restrictions or loss of access to this feature.

### How does entity collection differ between Version 1 and Version 2?¶

- Version 1: Requires separate definitions of entity collection logic and manages the complexity of mapping entities to business processes.
- Version 2: Integrates entity collection directly into the tool-calling framework. Instead of juggling separate systems, you define custom tools with parameters that naturally capture the entities needed.

### What types of tools does Version 2 include in its prompts?¶


Version 2 provides two types of tools:

- Document the purpose and structure of each Data Table.
- Regularly backup important data stored in Data Tables.
- Avoid storing JSON objects in the Data Tables.
- Frequent large-scale operations on Data Tables may impact AI Agent performance.
- Performing bulk operations is not recommended.

Data Definitionwhich includes:

- DefiningData TablesandViews.
- Granting accessto the apps, and processes for manipulating data from these Tables and Views.
- Defining Appsfor securely exporting and importing definitions and for querying the data from views or data tables.
- Data Manipulationallows performing CRUD operations on data from any of the data tables by makingAPI calls.

Data Manipulationallows performing CRUD operations on data from any of the data tables by makingAPI calls.

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/analytics/genai-analytics/genai-performance-analytics/


# Performance Analytics¶


The Performance Analytics page provides comprehensive insights into LLM performance across models, features, and requests. By analyzing success rates, token consumption, and response times, organizations can track accuracy, manage costs, and ensure system reliability. These analytics enable data-driven decisions for optimization and enhanced user experience through granular evaluation.

sources.content:
### Which LLM providers support tool calling?¶


All major LLM providers support some version of tool calling, including:

- Anthropic
- Cohere
- Google
- Mistral
- OpenAI
- Azure OpenAI

### What are the key components of a tool?¶


There are five key components to creating and using tools:

The Platform displays all the 100 plus languages that are supported. You can click on theConfigurebutton to enable a language.


Note:You can also search for a language by entering its name in the Search field.

There are five key components to creating and using tools:

- Name: A clear and descriptive name helps the LLM understand the tool's purpose.
- Description: The most crucial part; it explains what the tool can do, when to use it, and any limitations.
- Parameters: The specific pieces of information necessary for your tool to function.
- Action Nodes: The components that execute when the tool is called, including script nodes, service nodes, or search AI nodes.
- Transitions: Defines what happens after the tool executes, such as Default, Exit Out, or Jump to a Node.

Add a brief description of the use case context to guide the model.


Tools allow the Agent Node to interact with external services, fetching or posting data as needed. When called, they let language models perform tasks or obtain information by executing actions linked to Script, Service, or Search AI nodes. Users can add a maximum of 5 tools to each node.


Click+ Addto open theNew Toolcreation window. ReferTools Configurationfor details on defining tools.

The Platform offers three types of language models:

- System or Out-of-Box Integrations: Includes models from providers like OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, and Anthropic.
- Custom models (Bring Your Own Models): Allows users to bring their own models to the platform.
- Fine-tuned XO GPT models: Used for specific features like DialogGPT, Answer Generation, Query Rephrase, Rephrase Dialog Response, and Vector Generation.

### How do I configure out-of-box (OOB) integrations?¶


To configure OOB integrations:

- Actions: The Platform offers pre-built integrations with popular business applications to exchange data, such as Service Now, Salesforce, Zendesk, Hubspot, and many more. Each of these integrations provides a full conversation flow definition, a low-code interface for API integration, and pre-built response mapping and create dialog tasks with pre-built templates without need to worry about developer assistance.Learn more

Description of the API Calls Fields

# GitHub: Koredotcom/Public-APIs

Source: https://github.com/Koredotcom/Public-APIs

# Public-APIs---Postman-Collection
This repository contains the postman collection of all Public APIs provided by KORE.ai

- API Reference: The Platform allows you to develop AI Agent using secured APIs. All key activities like creation, import and export, training, and publishing are available using these APIs. Key account management activities like managing Platform access, managing admin, and roles can also be performed via secured public APIs.Learn more
- SDK Reference: The Platform offers Bots SDKs as a set of platform-specific client libraries that provide a quick and convenient way to get Platform capability into custom applications and provide you with more control over the assistants you build.Learn more

- fileId(required): Unique identifier of the uploaded file.
- fileName(optional): Name of the file for reference or display purposes.
- permissions(optional): Defines access control for the file.allowedUsers: List of user identifiers allowed to access the content.allowedGroups: List of groups whose members are allowed to access the content. These groups are saved as permission entities.
- allowedUsers: List of user identifiers allowed to access the content.
- allowedGroups: List of groups whose members are allowed to access the content. These groups are saved as permission entities.

- Passing the chunks fields in JSON format..
- Passing the details of the file uploaded for ingestion.
- Passing the web URLs to be crawled.
- When sourceType is `json`, use documents to pass structured content directly in the request. Each document object can include atitleand achunksarray. Each item in the chunks array must map exactly to the configured chunk fields (for example,chunkText,chunkTitle,recordUrl).

question: What should I infer from a medium-confidence answer versus a low-confidence one?

raw_response.answer:
⚠️ I'm not confident in this answer. Please verify with the source documents below.

Based on the provided context, it appears that the confidence level of an answer is related to the tone emotion detected in user input.

From the context, we can see that the "level" field has values ranging from +3 (definitely expressed) to -3 (definitely suppressed). The tone emotions are also listed with corresponding names, such as "angry", "disgust", and so on.

However, I couldn't find any explicit information on what to infer from a medium-confidence answer versus a low-confidence one. It seems that the confidence level is not explicitly defined in the context provided.

Therefore, I found missing information regarding the interpretation of confidence levels in Kore.ai's tone emotion detection system.

sources.content:
```
level
```

- +3– The user definitely expressed the tone emotion.
- +2– The user expressed the tone emotion.
- +1– The user likely expressed the tone emotion.
- 0– The user tone emotion is neutral.

### When to Use Deterministic Dialog Tasks¶


Choose deterministic flows when:

- Regulatory compliance is critical: Exact wording and predictable behavior are non-negotiable (e.g., legal disclaimers, financial disclosures).
- Audit trails are required: Every step must be traceable and reproducible.
- Simple, linear flows: The conversation follows a fixed path with minimal branching.
- Predictability is paramount: Consistent responses matter more than natural conversation.

Example scenarios: Account verification, payment processing, medical symptom triage, loan application forms, etc.

question: If the backend is unavailable, what does the user see?

raw_response.answer:
⚠️ I'm not confident in this answer. Please verify with the source documents below.

Based on the provided context, if the backend is unavailable, the user sees an "Error" status in the conversation. [Source 1]

The exact error message may vary depending on the specific error code returned by the API or the integration. However, the context does not provide information on what the user would see if a specific error occurs.

Additionally, the context mentions that actions can be authorized based on the authorization profiles supported by the third-party integrations, but it does not provide information on how this relates to an unavailable backend. [Source 7]

Therefore, without more information, it is unclear what the user would see if the backend is unavailable.

question: Can I tell whether a source came from GitHub or docs.kore.ai?

raw_response.answer:
⚠️ I'm not confident in this answer. Please verify with the source documents below.

To determine if a source came from GitHub or docs.kore.ai, you can look at the URL.

If the URL starts with "https://github.com/", it is likely from GitHub.
If the URL starts with "https://docs.kore.ai/", it is likely from docs.kore.ai.

I found that Sources 1 and 3 are from docs.kore.ai, while Sources 2 and 4 are from GitHub.

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/apis/quality-ai/api-list/


# Quality AI API List¶


The following table lists the APIs available for Quality AI.
Visit https://developer.kore.ai/docs/bots/sdks/using-the-botkit-sdk/  for configuration instructions and API documentation.

# Source: https://docs.kore.ai/xo/apis/agent-ai/api-list/


# Agent AI API List¶


The following table lists the APIs available for Agent AI.

# GitHub: Koredotcom/Public-APIs

Source: https://github.com/Koredotcom/Public-APIs

# Public-APIs---Postman-Collection
This repository contains the postman collection of all Public APIs provided by KORE.ai

# Kore.ai Web SDK for developers

Kore.ai SDK for web enables you to talk to Kore.ai bots over a web socket. This repo also comes with the code for sample application that developers can modify according to their Bot configuration.

#Supported Browsers
1. Google Chrome version 55 & above
2. Mozilla Firefox version 51 & above
3. Microsoft Edge version 111 & above
4. Mac Safari version 9.1 & above
5. Opera version 48 & above

#Supported JQuery version 3.3.1

# Setting up
